description:
GOAL: Verify that GC operates independently per-process. Each process's heap GCs when ITS allocator hits threshold; other processes keep running. Verification-heavy ticket — the design from .11.2 + .19.1 already gives us this; this ticket exercises and documents it.

## Decisions captured

- Per-process heap = bounded GC pause (GC time proportional to that process's heap size, not the whole system).
- Cooperative scheduling makes this trivial: a process can only GC at a safepoint where its roots are crawlable; cooperative yields ARE safepoints.
- Other processes keep running (their continuations stay valid; only the GC'd process pauses).

## Deliverables

- Trigger policy: per-heap allocation threshold (e.g. 75% full -> mark-sweep at next safepoint).
- Integration with .19.1 scheduler: after a process's continuation step, check its heap; if over threshold, run gc(roots from current frame chain) before re-scheduling.
- Documentation / tests demonstrating bounded-pause semantics.

## Tests

- Two processes; one allocates heavily, other does cheap work. The heavy process triggers GC; the cheap one keeps making progress.
- Single-process degenerate case still works.
- Heap fragmentation handled correctly under GC.

## Risks

- Cross-process pointers SHOULD NOT EXIST (deep-copy in .19.3 prevents them). If they ever do (e.g. via a bug), one process's GC could free another's data. Add an invariant assertion: a heap pointer in process A's frame never points into process B's heap.
- Transferable arenas (.19.5) are the only sanctioned cross-process pointer mechanism; their ownership transfer protocol must be robust.

## Out of scope

- Generational nursery (follow-up)
- Concurrent GC (follow-up)
title: Per-process independent GC (verification)
